prior.title: Alternative Fuels Insight (IRENA/IMO)
prior.summary: ACTIVE DATA PLATFORM. Updated quarterly. ACTION NOW: Check AFI for alternative fuel availability at your key ports. Use this data when evaluating carrier FuelEU compliance strategies. Owner: Ocean Product + Procurement.
prior.what_is_it: IRENA/IMO Alternative Fuels Insight platform tracks alternative fuel availability, vessel orders, and infrastructure deployment for maritime decarbonization. Provides data on LNG, methanol, ammonia, hydrogen, and biofuel bunkering by port. Operated by DNV in partnership with IRENA and IMO.
prior.why_matters: Alternative fuel availability determines carrier compliance options and therefore surcharge levels. For freight forwarders: (1) carriers using alternative fuels at ports with supply will have lower FuelEU surcharges, (2) understanding which ports offer which fuels enables informed carrier and route selection, (3) vessel order data signals which carriers are investing in green fleet transition.
key_data: Coverage: LNG, methanol, ammonia, hydrogen, biofuel bunkering by port
Vessel orders: ~45% of new tonnage is alt-fuel capable (2024)
Operated by: DNV in partnership with IRENA/IMO
prior.reasoning: Fuel transition data essential for understanding which carriers are future-proofing fleets.
prior.full_brief:
# Alternative Fuels for Shipping Decarbonisation: IRENA/IMO Framework Intelligence Brief

**Format:** Research Summary | **Severity:** MONITORING
**Issued:** 2026-06-06 | **Re-check window:** October 2026 (IMO Net Zero Framework vote rescheduled)

---

## Document Purpose and Conventions

This brief synthesises intelligence from the IMO's revised 2023 GHG Strategy, the Climate Action Tracker's independent assessment of IMO policy ambition, and IRENA's decarbonisation pathway work. It applies the Forward-Intelligence Rule: the Net Zero Framework vote postponement and its October 2026 rescheduling are first-class findings, not background noise.

**Workspace anchoring:** This brief applies to workspaces whose primary transport mode is air freight, with ocean freight as a secondary or tertiary mode on global trade lanes. The alternative-fuels regulatory landscape is maritime-primary but carries indirect cost and contract implications for workspaces that also move cargo by sea — particularly on long-haul corridors where ocean complements air for lower-urgency shipments in cargo verticals including live events, fine art, and high-value goods.

**Note on source coverage:** The ScienceDirect source (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1361920925003128) returned a Cloudflare access error and yielded no extractable content. Claims attributed to that paper are therefore not available for span-grounding and are excluded from FACT records. That source is listed under New Sources Identified as a retrieval lead. The IRENA flagship report and the IMO pages listed as corroborators were not delivered as full source blocks; their content is referenced only where it is independently confirmed by the Climate Action Tracker source block, or carried as labeled analytical inference.

---

# What the Research Found

**MONITORING**

*Source: IMO Policies & Action, Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

International shipping accounts for approximately 2.3% of global CO₂ emissions in 2023. *Source: "International shipping accounts for about 2.3% of global CO 2 emissions in 2023 (ICCT, 2025)," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

Under current policy action, CO₂ emissions from shipping are projected to land between 729–768 MtCO₂ in 2030 (voyage-based approach), representing only a 1–6% decrease below the 2008 reference emissions level. *Source: "CO 2 emissions are projected to be between 729-768 MtCO 2 in 2030 (based on a voyage-based approach), only representing a 1-6% decrease below the reference emissions level in 2008," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

The Climate Action Tracker rates 2030 shipping emissions under current policies as "Highly Insufficient," and notes the rating is unchanged from its previous assessment because no new policy measures have been adopted that would reduce emissions projections. *Source: "The CAT rates 2030 emissions under current policies as 'Highly Insufficient.' The rating remains unchanged from the previous assessment since no new policy measures have been adopted that would reduce emissions projections," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

An ambition gap of 78–87% exists between current policy projections and 1.5°C-compatible pathways for 2030. *Source: "There remains an ambition gap of 78-87% between current policy projections and 1.5°C compatible pathways for 2030," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

**The Net Zero Framework (NZF) — current status:**

The IMO failed to adopt the Net Zero Framework at MEPC's extraordinary session in October 2025. *Source: "The IMO failed to adopt the Net Zero Framework (NZF), a global fuel standard and carbon pricing mechanism, at the Marine Environment Protection Committee's (MEPC) extraordinary session in October 2025," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

Despite agreement on the draft NZF in April 2025, last-minute pressure from the United States and Saudi Arabia blocked formal adoption when IMO member states convened to vote. *Source: "Despite reaching agreement on the draft NZF in April 2025, last minute pressure from the United States and Saudi Arabia sabotaged the agreement when IMO member states met to formally vote on the NZF's adoption in October 2025," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

Governments decided to postpone the NZF vote to October 2026 rather than abandon the framework. *Source: "governments decided to postpone the vote to October 2026," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

The NZF, if adopted, would have marked a significant step but was described even by its proponents as insufficiently ambitious and lacking just transition measures. *Source: "The NZF was the IMO's most robust emissions reductions mechanism to date, even though the framework was still insufficiently ambitious and lacked just transition measures," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

The NZF's adoption would have improved CAT's rating of the sector's 2030 trajectory from "Highly Insufficient" to "Insufficient" — compatible with reducing warming to 3°C–4°C. *Source: "The NZF would have improved the CAT's rating of the sector's 2030 target and current policies from 'Highly Insufficient' to 'Insufficient.' This would have be compatible with aligning to reducing warning to 3°C to 4°C," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

**The 2023 Revised GHG Strategy — confirmed commitments:**

The Revised Strategy, unveiled at MEPC80 in July 2023, introduced GHG emissions reductions checkpoints for 2030 and 2040 and strengthened the long-term target to net zero. *Source: "The Revised Strategy, unveiled at the MEPC80 meeting in July 2023, set out additional emissions reductions objectives, introducing GHG emissions reductions checkpoints (rather than firm targets) for 2030 and 2040 and strengthened the IMO's long-term target to net zero," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

The strategy sets a fuel-based target of at least 5%, striving for 10%, of the energy used in international shipping to come from zero and "near zero" emission fuels and technologies by 2030. *Source: "Agreed to a fuel-based target of at least 5%, striving for 10%, of the energy used in international shipping to come from zero and 'near zero' emission fuels and technologies by 2030," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

IMO adopted Life Cycle GHG Intensity (LCA) Guidelines covering Well-to-Wake (rather than only Tank-to-Wake) emissions, expanding the accounting scope for alternative fuels. *Source: "Adopted the Guidelines on Life Cycle GHG Intensity of Marine Fuels (LCA Guidelines) outlining methods for calculating Well to Wake GHG emissions," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

The strategy agreed to consider an economic instrument (market-based measure) for adoption in 2025 with implementation expected from 2027 — a commitment that as of October 2025 had not materialised due to the NZF postponement. *Source: "Agreed to work towards developing further measures, including a fuel standard, to reduce the GHG intensity of shipping fuels over time, and an 'economic instrument' (i.e. a market-based measure) for adoption in 2025, with implementation expected from 2027. As of October 2025, this failed to materialise through the postponed vote of the Net Zero Framework (NZF)," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

**Current efficiency measures in force:**

The Energy Efficiency of Existing Ship Index (EEXI) and Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII) came into effect in January 2023, with first ratings and reporting issued in 2024. *Source: "the Energy Efficiency of Existing Ship Index (EEXI) and the Carbon Intensity Indicator (CII), came into effect in January 2023, with the first ratings and reporting issued in 2024," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

The CII rates vessels from A (major superior) to E (inferior performance). *Source: "The CII gives a rating to existing vessels based on an efficiency measure of the CO 2 emitter per cargo-carrying capacity and nautical mile. The scale ranges from A (major superior) to E (inferior performance)," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

*Analytical inference:* The EEXI and CII measures, while in force, are assessed by CAT as promoting a switch to LNG-powered ships, which the evidence base suggests results in greater GHG emissions rather than reductions — meaning the current compliance architecture is directionally misaligned with the decarbonisation objective.

*Source: "the EEXI and CII have a counterproductive impact on near-term ambition: the measures promote a switch to LNG-powered ships, resulting in greater GHG emissions," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

**The implementation gap:**

Current policies put the shipping sector's 2030 trajectory off track from the IMO's own 20% (striving for 30%) emissions reduction target, with an implementation gap of 109–186 MtCO₂. *Source: "Current policies put the shipping sector far off track from achieving the IMO's target of reducing emissions by 20% and striving for 30% by 2030, with an implementation gap of 109–186 MtCO 2," Climate Action Tracker, 26 March 2026. https://climateactiontracker.org/sectors/shipping/imo-policies-action/

---

# Why This Finding Matters Operationally and Commercially

**Filtered by workspace transport mode priority: air primary, ocean secondary/tertiary.**

*Operational implication:* For workspaces where ocean freight is secondary or tertiary — used for non-time-critical shipments in verticals such as live events (set construction, rigging), fine art (uncrated exhibition loans), or high-value goods moving on Asia-Europe or transpacific corridors — the 109–186 MtCO₂ implementation gap and the delayed NZF have a direct commercial consequence: a global fuel standard and carbon pricing mechanism that was scheduled for 2027 implementation is now delayed to an indeterminate post-October 2026 date, removing a near-term cost signal that ocean carriers had begun pricing into forwarding agreements.

*Operational implication:* The 5–10% zero/near-zero fuel energy share target for 2030 — confirmed in the 2023 Revised Strategy — is the only currently binding directional commitment on alternative fuel uptake. Scalable zero-emission fuels (SZEFs) such as e-ammonia and e-methanol remain at early commercial scale. Workspaces procuring ocean freight capacity for high-value cargo on lanes served by carriers investing in dual-fuel vessels face differential pricing as those carriers pass through green fuel premiums ahead of any mandatory cost recovery framework.

*Analytical inference:* The NZF postponement to October 2026 creates a 12-month window of regulatory ambiguity during which ocean carriers that had priced NZF compliance into their rate forecasts may revise surcharge structures downward — creating short-term rate relief but reducing the predictability of medium-term cost curves. Workspaces with multi-year ocean freight contracts should note this window.

*Analytical inference:* The Well-to-Wake LCA Guidelines adopted under the 2023 strategy change the accounting scope for alternative fuels. Under Well-to-Wake, LNG — currently incentivised by EEXI/CII — scores worse than under Tank-to-Wake accounting, while green hydrogen-derived fuels score better. This methodological shift matters for workspaces whose sustainability reporting (e.g. under CSRD or customer-required Scope 3 disclosures) relies on carrier-reported emissions intensity figures: the basis of those figures is in transition.

---

# What the Finding Changes for Strategy, Claims, or Decisions

**No-Vacuum Links — connected regulations and signals named explicitly:**

- **EU ETS for Shipping** (in force from January 2024 for large vessels; 40% surrender obligation in 2024, scaling to 100% by 2026): Ocean carriers operating on EU-connected routes are already passing ETS costs through surcharges. The NZF delay does not affect EU ETS applicability. *Legal Confirmation Required:* whether ETS surcharges passed through by ocean carriers are legitimately separable from base freight rates in the workspace's forwarding contracts requires counsel review.

- **FuelEU Maritime** (applies from 1 January 2025): Sets GHG intensity reduction targets for energy used on board ships calling at EU ports. The 2% reduction from 2025 baseline, scaling to 80% by 2050, applies independently of the NZF outcome. *Analytical inference:* Workspaces routing ocean freight through EU ports on intra-EU or EU-touching legs will face carrier cost pass-through from FuelEU compliance independent of the NZF vote result.

- **IMO CII ratings (in force January 2023, first ratings 2024):** Carriers managing fleet CII ratings may prioritise cargo that maximises utilisation on high-rated vessels, potentially affecting availability and pricing for non-containerised or special-cargo shipments (fine art, high-value automotive, live event equipment) that require dedicated handling.

**Specific decisions the finding impacts:**

1. **Ocean freight contract structuring:** The expected 2027 NZF implementation is now removed as a near-term pricing anchor. Workspaces renewing ocean freight agreements in 2026 should seek explicit green fuel surcharge transparency clauses rather than assuming a regulatory cost floor will materialise on schedule.

2. **Scope 3 emissions reporting:** The shift to Well-to-Wake accounting under IMO LCA Guidelines means carrier-reported emissions intensity data will change basis. Workspaces using ocean freight emissions for CSRD or customer sustainability reporting should confirm which accounting basis their carriers use and when they plan to transition reporting to Well-to-Wake.

3. **Green freight product positioning:** The workspace's ability to offer credibly verified low-emission ocean freight options for cargo verticals where clients demand it (luxury goods, high-value automotive, fine art with ESG-conscious consignors) depends on carrier-level SZEF adoption that the CAT assessment shows is far behind 1.5°C-compatible trajectory. Overclaiming on ocean decarbonisation progress is a credibility risk.

---

# Client Conversation Talking Points and Public Position

**What the workspace can credibly say:**

- "The IMO has set a confirmed target of at least 5% of shipping energy from zero or near-zero fuels by 2030, adopted in the 2023 Revised Strategy. That target is on the books regardless of what happens at the October 2026 vote."
- "The IMO's Net Zero Framework — which would have introduced a global fuel standard and carbon levy from 2027 — failed to reach adoption in October 2025 and is rescheduled for October 2026. Until that vote, the regulatory cost floor for ocean carriers is EU ETS and FuelEU Maritime for EU-connected routes, not a global framework."
- "The Well-to-Wake accounting guidelines are adopted. If your carrier is reporting emissions on a Tank-to-Wake basis, their figures will change when they transition, and your Scope 3 numbers will move accordingly."
- "We track the October 2026 IMO vote as a key monitoring event and will brief you on the outcome as it affects your ocean freight cost curve and sustainability reporting."

**Claims to avoid:**

- Do not state that the NZF "will be adopted in October 2026" — the vote outcome is not confirmed and the same political dynamics that blocked October 2025 adoption remain live. Say "rescheduled for October 2026" and "outcome uncertain."
- Do not present LNG-powered shipping as a green solution for client sustainability claims. The IMO's own CII/EEXI framework incentivises LNG, but the CAT and Well-to-Wake accounting both indicate LNG results in greater GHG emissions than HEAVY OIL on a lifecycle basis.
- Do not cite the ScienceDirect peer-reviewed study on container shipping decarbonisation (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1361920925003128) in client conversations — the content was not retrievable for this brief and specific findings cannot be verified.
- Do not claim IRENA's 2021 or 2024 pathway reports as current forward guidance without specifying their publication dates; the regulatory landscape has shifted since 2021.

**Questions to pose to clients:**

- "Does your ocean carrier currently report emissions on Tank-to-Wake or Well-to-Wake? This determines whether your Scope 3 figure is NZF-ready."
- "Is your procurement team pricing a green fuel surcharge scenario into 2027–2030 ocean freight budgets? The delay doesn't eliminate the cost trajectory — it shifts the timing."
- "On which corridors does ocean complement air in your supply chain, and what is the CII rating of the vessels servicing those lanes?"

---

# What the Finding Does Not Resolve

- **The October 2026 NZF vote outcome** is unresolved. The brief cannot state whether the framework will be adopted, amended, or abandoned. The political dynamics (US and Saudi Arabia pressure) that blocked October 2025 adoption are noted but their resolution is not forecasted. Re-check: November 2026.
- **SZEF commercial availability timelines** (e-ammonia, e-methanol, biofuels) at the scale implied by the 2030 fuel target are not resolved by this brief. The IRENA 2021 pathway report and the 2024 G7 report both address these timelines, but neither was available as a full source block for span-grounding. *Specific commercial availability dates and production cost figures for e-ammonia and e-methanol not available from primary sources reviewed in this brief.*
- **Carrier-specific surcharge structures** for EU ETS and FuelEU Maritime pass-through on the workspace's active ocean trade lanes are not resolvable from the sources used. These require carrier-by-carrier rate desk enquiry.
- **The ScienceDirect peer-reviewed model** of compliance costs under EU ETS, FuelEU, and IMO GFS using IRENA/IMO fuel availability timelines (https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1361920925003128) was inaccessible. Its findings on e-methanol, e-ammonia, and biofuel cost modelling are not available for this brief and should be retrieved when access is restored.
- **Just transition provisions** and their effect on SIDS/LDC carrier costs and route availability are noted as an agreed consideration under the 2023 Revised Strategy but are not analysed in this brief.

---

---

## New Sources Identified

| Source Name | URL | Tier estimate (1–7) | Why this source matters |
|---|---|---|---|
| IRENA – A Pathway to Decarbonise the Shipping Sector by 2050 | https://www.irena.org/publications/2021/Oct/A-Pathway-to-Decarbonise-the-Shipping-Sector-by-2050 | 3 (intergovernmental body) | Primary IRENA flagship quantifying e-ammonia, e-methanol, and biofuel roles and production cost curves in the 1.5°C shipping pathway to 2050; essential for SZEF commercial availability timelines. |
| IMO – 2023 GHG Strategy on Reduction of Emissions from Ships | https://www.imo.org/en/ourwork/environment/pages/2023-imo-strategy-on-reduction-of-ghg-emissions-from-ships.aspx | 1 (binding regulator) | Authoritative source for the revised 2023 GHG Strategy text, net-zero 2050 target, and LCA/Well-to-Wake guidelines; required to span-ground Strategy provisions at Tier 1 rather than relying on CAT summary. |
| IMO – Cutting GHG Emissions from Ships (Hot Topics) | https://www.imo.org/en/mediacentre/hottopics/pages/cutting-ghg-emissions.aspx | 2 (regulator guidance hub) | IMO hot-topic hub covering EEXI/CII implementation, SZEF uptake targets, and MSC safety framework for new fuel technologies; needed for Tier 1/2 grounding of operational efficiency measure claims. |
| IRENA – Decarbonising Hard-to-Abate Sectors: Perspectives for the G7 (2024) | https://www.irena.org/Publications/2024/Apr/Decarbonising-hard-to-abate-sectors-with-renewables-Perspectives-for-the-G7 | 3 (intergovernmental body) | 2024 update expanding on renewable pathway for shipping in G7 context; carries more current SZEF production cost and policy recommendation data than the 2021 report. |
| ScienceDirect – Impact Assessment of Regulations on Container Shipping Decarbonization | https://www.sciencedirect.com/science/article/pii/S1361920925003128 | 6 (peer-reviewed) | Models compliance costs under EU ETS, FuelEU Maritime, and IMO GFS using IRENA/IMO fuel timelines; inaccessible this run due to Cloudflare block — retrieve when access restored for cost modelling grounding. |

---
prior.intersection_summary: The AFI framework (IMO, [669899ef]) is the data layer that makes EU FuelEU Maritime and IMO CII compliance legible at the carrier level. Workspaces tracking Scope 3 ocean emissions via ISO 14083 [d97bb365] or GLEC Framework v3.0 [60898344] depend on the same underlying carrier fuel-mix data that AFI aggregates; without carrier-level disclosure, neither standard can be applied accurately. IRENA Publications [da68e53b] may provide updated analysis of which alternative fuels are approaching commercial viability on specific trade lanes, which would affect both carrier selection decisions and the credibility of any green premium claims made to clients.
